title: Präpositionen + Dativ
explainer_md:
These prepositions **always** take the Dativ: **[[aus]]** (out of/from), **[[bei]]** (at/near), **[[mit]]** (with), **[[nach]]** (after/to), **[[seit]]** (since/for), **[[von]]** (from/of), **[[zu]]** (to).

Many learners sing them to a melody: *aus – bei – mit – nach – seit – von – zu*. However you memorize them: after these words the next article is **always** Dativ.

| | m | f | n | pl |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dativ | dem / einem | der / einer | dem / einem | den … -n |

*Ich fahre mit **dem** Bus.* — I go by bus.

*Sie kommt aus **der** Türkei.* — She comes from Turkey.

*Nach **der** Schule spiele ich Fußball.* — After school I play football.

*Er wohnt bei **seinen** Eltern.* — He lives with his parents.

**Handy contractions** you'll hear everywhere: zu + dem = **zum**, zu + der = **zur**, bei + dem = **beim**, von + dem = **vom**. *Ich gehe zum [[Arzt]].* — I'm going to the doctor.

**Tip:** *seit* is used for things that started in the past and still continue — with the present tense: *Ich lerne **seit einem** Jahr Deutsch.* — I've been learning German for a year.

payload.explanation: „[[mit]]“ always takes the Dativ. Masculine: der → dem (der Bus).
„[[aus]]“ always takes the Dativ. Feminine: die → der (die Türkei).

payload.prompt: Nach ___ Schule gehe ich nach Hause.
payload.explanation: „[[nach]]“ always takes the Dativ. Feminine: die → der (die Schule).
„[[bei]]“ always takes the Dativ. Plural: die → den (den Eltern).
„[[zu]]“ takes the Dativ; zu + dem contracts to „zum“ (der Arzt).
„[[seit]]“ always takes the Dativ. Neuter: ein → einem (das Jahr).
„[[von]]“ takes the Dativ; von + dem contracts to „vom“ (der Bäcker).
„[[bei]]“ takes the Dativ; bei + dem contracts to „beim“ (der Friseur).
„[[mit]]“ always takes the Dativ, also with pronouns: ich → mir.
„[[zu]]“ takes the Dativ. Plural: unsere → unseren (die Großeltern).

payload.prompt: Ich gehe mit ___ Schwester ins Kino. (meine)
payload.explanation: „[[mit]]“ always takes the Dativ. Feminine: meine → meiner.
„[[aus]]“ always takes the Dativ. Feminine: die → der.
„[[nach]]“ always takes the Dativ. Neuter: das → dem.
„[[seit]]“ always takes the Dativ. Masculine: ein → einem.

payload.prompt: Das ist ein Geschenk von ___ Oma. (meine)
payload.explanation: „[[von]]“ always takes the Dativ. Feminine: meine → meiner.
„[[zu]]“ takes the Dativ. Plural: unsere → unseren, and the noun adds -n (Freunden).
„[[mit]]“ takes the Dativ: der → dem. The verb stands in second position.
„[[aus]]“ takes the Dativ. Feminine: die → der (die Türkei).

payload.translation: I have been living here for a year.
payload.explanation: „[[seit]]“ takes the Dativ: ein → einem (das Jahr). The time phrase and „[[hier]]“ can come in either order.
„[[bei]]“ takes the Dativ. Plural: ihre → ihren (die Eltern).

payload.translation: He has been ill for a week.
payload.explanation: „[[seit]]“ takes the Dativ. Feminine: eine → einer (die Woche).

reasons: It follows „mit“, which always takes the Dativ
It is the direct object of the verb
It is the subject of the sentence
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[mit]]“ is a Dativ preposition, so der Bus becomes dem Bus.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „aus“, which always takes the Dativ
It is the direct object of the verb
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[aus]]“ is a Dativ preposition. Feminine: die → der (die Türkei).

reasons: It is the direct object of the verb
It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „nach“, which always takes the Dativ
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[nach]]“ is a Dativ preposition. Feminine: die → der (die Schule).

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „aus“, which always takes the Dativ
It is the direct object of the verb
It is the indirect object (recipient)
payload.explanation: Der Zug is the one arriving → subject → Nominativ. Only „Berlin“ follows the Dativ preposition „[[aus]]“.
„[[mit]]“ + Dativ: das Fahrrad → dem Fahrrad.

payload.prompt: Sie wohnt seit ___ Woche in Berlin.
payload.explanation: „[[seit]]“ + Dativ: die Woche → einer Woche.
zu + der (feminine Dativ) contracts to zur: die Bäckerei → zur Bäckerei.
zu + dem (masculine Dativ) contracts to zum.
„[[nach]]“ + Dativ: die Arbeit → der Arbeit.
zum = zu + dem (masculine Dativ).

payload.translation: I have been learning German for a month.
payload.explanation: „[[seit]]“ + Dativ, and after the fronted time phrase the verb stays in position 2.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It is the direct object of „wohnen“
It follows „bei“, which always takes the Dativ
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[bei]]“ always takes the Dativ — plural: seinen Großeltern (noun already ends in -n).
„[[mit]]“ always takes the Dativ. Masculine: der → dem (der Ball).
„[[aus]]“ always takes the Dativ. Neuter: das → dem (das Haus).

payload.prompt: Ich habe das Buch von ___ Freundin. (meine)
payload.explanation: „[[von]]“ takes the Dativ. Feminine: meine → meiner (die Freundin).
„[[zu]]“ takes the Dativ; zu + dem contracts to „zum“ (das Schwimmbad).

payload.prompt: Nach ___ Ferien beginnt die Schule. (die, Plural)
payload.explanation: „[[nach]]“ takes the Dativ. Plural: die → den (die Ferien).
„[[von]]“ always takes the Dativ, also with pronouns: sie → ihr.
„[[aus]]“ always takes the Dativ. Masculine: der → dem.
„[[bei]]“ always takes the Dativ. Feminine: ihre → ihrer.
zu + der (feminine Dativ) contracts to „zur“ (die Post).

payload.prompt: Fährst du mit ___ Kollegen zur Arbeit? (deine, Plural)
payload.explanation: „[[mit]]“ takes the Dativ. Plural: deine → deinen (die Kollegen).

payload.translation: He is coming out of the cinema.
payload.explanation: „[[aus]]“ takes the Dativ. Neuter: das → dem (das Kino).

payload.translation: After breakfast I go to school.
payload.explanation: „[[nach]]“ takes the Dativ (dem Frühstück); zur = zu + der. After the fronted phrase the verb stays in position 2.

payload.translation: She has been working at the dentist's for two months.
payload.explanation: „[[seit]]“ takes the Dativ — the plural noun adds -n (Monaten). beim = bei + dem.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It is the direct object of the verb
It follows „von“, which always takes the Dativ
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[von]]“ is a Dativ preposition. Feminine: meine → meiner (die Oma).

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „mit“, which always takes the Dativ
It is the direct object of the verb
It is the indirect object (recipient)
payload.explanation: Die Lehrerin is the one speaking → subject → Nominativ. Only „dem Schüler“ follows the Dativ preposition „[[mit]]“.
